35.text: Toverland is an immensely beautiful park, filled with lush greenery and pleasant views that make it easy to disconnect from everything outside the gates. The immersion is only briefly interrupted by what we have affectionately labeled "Old Toverland"—the two massive concrete halls that house the park’s original indoor attractions. What they lack on the outside, they more than make up for within. Both halls are impressively themed and manage to blend indoor attractions with outdoor rides in a way that feels natural rather than forced. It is a unique look, and one that works surprisingly well. Toverland leans heavily into fantasy theming, and while it could use some more variation, many of the areas (especially the newest ones) look incredible and sell the fantasy really well. Overall, Toverland is a visually stunning park.

Without Troy, Toverland’s coaster lineup becomes significantly less exciting. Fēnix is a very strong B&M wing coaster, but there is a big drop-off down to the park's family spinner, Dwervelwind, even though it is a fun ride. Booster Bike is an okay family-thrill coaster with a good launch but a very simple layout. While Toos-Express is pretty cool for a smaller family coaster with its fun drop and indoor setting, the depth of the lineup disappears very quickly after the top two rides. They simply need more coasters to become truly competitive against other international parks. Until then, we know exactly where we're going to be spending our time at Toverland.

We visited Toverland on July 18, 2025, for what we thought was a simple warm-up park on a longer coaster trip. Instead, it turned out to be the biggest surprise of the trip. Toverland, where all roads lead to Troy, earned its reputation as a rock-solid theme park, home to two absolute powerhouses in Fēnix and Troy. Combine that with smooth operations and a complete lack of major weaknesses, and you get a park that feels tight, polished, and full of charm. This is a park we would absolutely love to return to.

Outside of coasters and water rides, Toverland offers a nice blend of theme park classics, such as a pirate ship and teacups, but they also have a few more unique attractions like Maximus' Blitz Bahn and the big Dragonwatch tower ride. They also sport a Gerstlauer Sky Roller and a classic themed boat ride, 'Merlin's Quest'.

Overall, Toverland does a good job of covering the basics while offering a few standout family rides. However, they really lack in both the thrill and dark ride departments. The Sky Roller is the closest thing you get to a thrill flat ride, and Merlin's Quest is the closest to a proper dark ride, but neither quite manages to fill the shoes of the real deal. Therefore, adding one of these types of attractions would really help elevate Toverland's lineup to the next level.

We arrived at our first-ever GCI with some expectations but without really knowing what kind of ride it would be. Troy absolutely shattered them. This wooden beast wastes no time, diving straight into a relentless layout packed with airtime, blistering speed, and great intensity from start to finish. It's raw, crazy, and completely unapologetic in what it does. There is no doubt in our minds that this is one of the best wooden coasters we've ever ridden, and the GCI experience is vastly different from what other manufacturers offer. While Troy stops just short of a perfect 10 due to a lack of refinement, it easily earns a rock-solid 9 and firmly anchors the entire park.

As for water rides, Toverland offers a very good log flume that splits its time between indoor and outdoor sections, featuring a layout that utilizes both strong theming and a tilted turntable for extra thrills. Unfortunately, the rapids ride is just okay and feels far closer to a scenic lazy river than the kind of thrilling rapids we prefer. It is undeniably pretty, but leaving a rapids ride as dry as when we boarded is a definite negative for us. Toverland would benefit heavily from a third water ride, specifically one that focuses on thrills and getting riders wet.

We found ourselves relying heavily on the park app, as the layout remains fairly confusing and unintuitive, likely due to years of expansion. Thankfully, a network of small shortcuts makes navigating much easier once you discover them. After a while, we were crisscrossing the park between Fēnix and Troy with increasing confidence. Strangely, no matter which route we took, we always seemed to end up at Troy, and honestly, that felt more like a feature than a flaw.

As tradition dictates, we went straight for a burger, and Toverland did not disappoint. A soft brioche bun paired with a juicy patty, crisp bacon, and parmesan flakes made for a surprisingly strong meal. Adding a side of well-tasting loaded fries into the equation and we land at a very respectable score. The rating is only held back slightly by the somewhat limited variety across the park when compared to giants like Europa-Park or food-focused parks like Tivoli Gardens, but overall, the food we got at Toverland, was very good.

Arriving on a hot July day, our first stop was the waterside bar near the show arena, and holy Gerstlauer, did it deliver. These were without question the most refreshing, thirst-quenching, and genuinely delicious mocktails we have ever had in a theme park. If you find yourself here, skipping these would be like going to DisneySea and ignoring the Little Green Alien mochi. Trust us, this is a game-changer. The remaining variety of snacks was, just like food, somewhat limited to standard theme park fare, but some classic soft serve ice cream alongside those god-sent drinks, and we were more than satisfied.

There were no major ride closures during our visit, with solid dispatches and multi-train operations across the board. It is hard not to score the park well here. That said, as always, we would like to see how they perform under truly heavy crowds before pushing the score into the higher 8s and 9s. Some stacking was observed on Troy in particular, leaving us comfortable with a 7.5.

Toverland is a very well-run park, staffed by friendly and outgoing employees who make it easy to focus on enjoying the day rather than logistics. The merchandise selection is also strong, something we never take for granted. Phantasialand, if you are reading this, please take notes. Everything about the park suggests a genuine care for the guest experience, and it caps off what was an excellent and memorable visit.

36.text: Walibi is a very beautiful park, with a massive lake at its center that elevates both navigation and overall atmosphere. The calmness of this centerpiece is quickly contrasted by the controlled chaos surrounding it, especially in the area around Kondaa, which is a genuinely impressive plaza given to their top coaster. The park has several exotic themed areas that contrast nicely against each other and while you will not find Disney level immersion here, that is not what Walibi is trying to be. It's doing its own thing, and it does it well.

We visited Walibi on July 19th 2025 for our first visit ever. We were very excited to try the big coaster line-up of this park and Kondaa was especially a huge draw for us. Walibi Belgium delivers big thrills with a healthy dose of character and sports one of Europe's best coasters and some crazy water rides. It is not the smoothest park experience out there, and it stumbles in areas but when it works, it really works. Overall we had a great time at Belgiums premier themepark!

Walibi offers a solid dark ride in Challenge of Tutankhamon, but unfortunately we missed out on Popcorn Revenge. As for flat rides, the park covers the basics with a decent Top Spin in Buzzsaw and a standard Wave Swinger. It also has a large Intamin drop tower in "Dalton Terror". Overall, the quality is solid but there is plenty of room for improvements, Afterall, this is not the main reason you come to Walibi Belgium and that is perfectly fine.

Let us not overthink this. Kondaa is a beast. Immense airtime defines this ride from start to finish, with an insane first drop, truly magnificent moments of airtime. relentless pace and wild transitions. This coaster was designed by someone who clearly hates gravity, and it's all wrapped in an ultra smooth experience with some of the best coaster trains in the business. Kondaa leaves very little to be desired, which is why it earns a 10 on our scale!

Smoothies were good. Soft ice was fine. Pancakes were a disaster. That about sums it up.
We waited all day for the pancake house to open, only to be met with a stack of dry, pre cooked pancakes hidden under a sheet of foil. When a park cannot spare thirty seconds to make a fresh pancake on request, it leaves a bad taste in our mouths (literally). Selection was very standard and overall quality was okay but we hope to see the park improve in this category in the future.

This category turned out to be one of the biggest surprises of the trip. Walibi Belgium might quietly house some of the best water rides in Europe (Yes, Chiapas we see you), assuming you are not afraid of going beyond wet and straight into fully soaked territory. The log flume "Flash Back" is a very good flume with a turntable and tunnel sections. Pulsar, a water coaster hybrid, combines real thrills with equally real drenching. And then there is the parks rapids "Radja River", which we are still convinced violated at least three safety standards. At times, we genuinely think we would have exited drier had someone simply hosed us down. A truly mighty, and very wet, trio.

As ParkRating season 1 & 2 tradition dictates, we always have to try a burger, purely for scientific reasons. Walibi delivered something on par with a McDonalds quality burger, featuring a soft bun and a patty of familiar size. That said, it was perfectly decent, and the overall food selection across the park was solid enough. Nothing particularly stood out and it very much seems like to Six Flags spirit lives on here but the food game is perfectly functional and will keep you fueled during your visit.

The park is laid out in a simple circular pattern around the central lake, and it works. Everything is fairly easy to find, walking distances are reasonable, and signage does its job. The downsides are a lack of shaded or seated areas and an app that borders on unusable. The area where their new coaster is going was under construction during our visit which did cut off some navigation options but overall, navigating the park was refreshingly straightforward.

Kondaa ran like an absolute dream. Most rides featured solid dispatch times, and staff were generally on point throughout the day. However, breakdowns on Cobra and several on Pulsar and a - at times - impractical Speedy Pass system, particularly on Vampire and Calamity Mine, where being routed through exits led to some awkward moments when groups had planned to ride together, kept this score from reaching the top. Still, overall efficiency remained solid and operations were good.

This is the category where Walibi truly failed. The entrance experience was needlessly clunky and chaotic, parking was confusing, and the overall arrival experience felt unnecessarily frustrating. While we could forgive all of that, the stall management was borderline unacceptable. On a hot summer day, we struggled to buy even basic necessities like drinks or ice cream. Many locations were closed until late evening, despite the app claiming otherwise.
The saving graces were a strong merchandise selection and non staggered ride openings, which are the only reasons this category avoids dipping even lower. While the park is overall a great time, be ready for some potentially frustrations during your visit.

As we quickly learned, Kondaa stands far above the rest of the lineup, and when it comes to evaluating what surrounds it, we are left wanting more. Psyké Underground is a delightfully experience, and both Tiki Waka and Calamity Mine serve as strong family coasters. What ultimately holds this category back is Walibi’s darker side, or rather, its old Vekoma side. Loup-Garou is a fun but headache inducing woody, and Vampire is a not-so-fun SLC that will have you wishing you could go back to just having a headache. We are very curious to see how this category evolves once Mecalodon opens.

37.text: We visited Phantasialand on July 20th, 2024, with very high expectations for our first time at the park. It more than delivered. The theming, immersion, and attention to detail felt on par with what you would expect from Disneyland or Universal Studios, only here, the world-class dark rides are replaced with great coasters and thrilling flat rides. One big disclaimer is that we did not travel alone and therefore this was not a true Parkrating trip, which meant that we only had one day at the park which was more relaxed and less ride-focused than usual. As a result, we missed out on several re-rides and our data is not quite as good as usual but hey, guess that mean we have to come back, right?

The category that we found the hardest to rate was "Best Coaster" since it's the one that was hit the hardest by the type of trip we were on. We only got two rides on F.L.Y and a single on Taron and Black Mamba, which is far below our standard for such notable rides. In the end we went with F.L.Y as the best coaster given its Extremely smooth ride experience, immersive theming and how it majestically soars around Rookburgh. 

F.L.Y is a state-of-the-art coaster with a cool loading procedure and a  unique launch in the flying position. The ride experience itself wasn't that fantastic and the flying position is a bit awkward and we weren't exactly blown away but we are very curious to get back to this coaster and see what it really can on a proper Parkrating trip.

The supporting coaster line-up of Phantasialand is one of quality and variation.
If F.L.Y is the best coaster in the park, Taron and Black Mamba are 2 formidable supporting coasters to have. Taron with its long, smooth, winding track, offering many whippy turns and airtime moments. Black Mamba with its intense inversions and powerful ride experience. Too bad we only got to try these two great coasters once.
Winjas, Crazy Bats and Colorado adventure are all 3 of the best family coasters we've tried while brining completely different ride experiences.
You do not want to miss out on the coasters of this park.

Phantasialand's biggest strength is obvious as soon as you get to the park. The theming is some of the most immersive we have seen delivering an otherworldly level of quality and consistency. But more so than just the quantity and quality of the theming, Phantasialand's areas are a lot different and a whole lot more unique than the usual western and simple fantasy themes you see at most european themeparks, giving a truly unique and immerive feeling while traversing the park. It also makes the park feel a lot bigger and richer than its somewhat modest size and amount of attractions.

We had lunch at Uhrwerk, and as is tradition, we had to order a burger. It lived up to expectations, easily becoming the best theme park burger we have tried since this slightly odd obsession began. As the gallery shows, it might not look the part, but it packed a punch. Add the fact that FLY flies by just outside, along with the beautiful theming of the restaurant, and we are bound to rate it highly. Let us hope the rest of the park's dining follows suit when we visit again later.

We had high hopes after trying the burger at uhrwreck, and the snack game mostly delivered. The mocktails were great, and the variety was solid across the park. That said, no soft ice with churros was a letdown. With a few more iconic snack pairings, this score could have gone even higher.

As we have come to accept, the price of immersion is navigation. Phantasialand is filled with twisted paths and overlap in ways that make sense thematically but not always practically. In addition ride entrances are sometimes hard to find, and rest areas are rare. The app and well placed screens in the park help, but it takes some effort.

Phantasialand operated like a well-oiled machine, with efficient dispatches and proactive queue management throughout. The only hiccup? Taron was down when we arrived in the morning, which definitely gave us a good scare. Luckily, it opened after an hour or so and from there, we didn't experience anything but fantastic operations.

Phantasialand ran a tight ship with no facility closures and a polished overall experience. But they did have a major ride closed the entire day in Talocan and it's also baffling to us that a park this rich in detail and immersion offers little in the way of merchandise,  unless you're in the market for a waving plastic cat.

It did not take long for us to agree on a second perfect 10. Chiapas is the best log flume we have been on and is arguably the best of its kind in the world. River Quest is wild, chaotic, and completely its own thing. The only thing missing? A third water ride, then we might have broken the scale.

38.text: We were equally as impressed with how they ran all 3 water rides on a cold October day, as we were disappointed with both Thor's Hammer and SpinSpider being closed the entire day. The parking situation (where to park and how to pay for it) was also quite confusing but there were no amenity closures and the park had some decent merch so overall, not bad.

We visited Tusenfryd on october 4th 2024 for our first ever visit. We weren't really sure what to expect from norways biggest theme park but our misty halloween visit to this nature-based park on the side of a mountain turned out to be a very solid one that left us with many opinions about the park.

Tusenfryd's appearance is one of two tales. On one hand you got the insanely pretty nature that does a lot of good for the park like how Speed monster utilizes the hillside to boost its ride experience or how many times you can get some truly breathtaking views over the park and its surrounding areas. On the other hand, the park is fairly light on theming and we couldn't help but to feel like Route 66 which was the area with the most theming, felt slightly outdated. Immersion wasn't particularly high and if this park would have resided on a flat piece of land it might have gotten a fairly middling score but the natural beauty and the effect it has on the park experience, cannot be denied, so overall the park actually does quite well here.

The best coaster at Tusenfryd is Storm - The Dragon Legend. This little inverted Gerstlauer thrill coaster is a small but mighty ride that packs one hell of a punch! While the ride is very short, you will get treated to some freaky hangtime, strong positives and one downright violent pop of airtime (and we mean that as a compliment). It is a somewhat incomplete ride, especially considering its short duration but it's absolutely a coaster worthy of the title as Norways best coaster and a must ride for any thrill seeker.

Tusenfryd has three supporting thrill coasters. Speed monster and Thundercoaster are both solid rides, Speed Monster which is smooth and fun and ThunderCoaster which is quite rough but has a great layout with some solid forces throughout. Loopen is an old Vekoma Tornado coaster which offered a nice vertical loop and a novelty experience but that's about it. Apart from a spinning kiddie ride from Zamperla, the parks only family coaster is Western-Expressen which we sadly didn't get to experience due to it being down for most of the day but this park desperately needs another major family coaster.

One thing you will learn very quickly when visiting Tusenfryd is how much walking you're going to be doing and it's not just walking simple, straight paths either. The park consist of a couple of fairly flat areas, all located at very different heights and the paths connecting them are long, windy and fairly steep. It doesn't help that the area where the water rides and dark ride is, is located in its own secluded area with only one way in and out, and that path is brutal so if you missed some rides there or want to go back for some re-rides, it's really going to drain you. Simply but, Tusenfryd is many thing, but practical, it is not.

It's quite impressive for such a northern park to have 3 water rides and this park definitely did better here than we thought it would. While the old arrow flume ride "Tømmerstupet" was very forgettable, SuperSplash was a simple but fun water coaster from Mack. The standout water ride however, has to go to their rapids "Ragnarok". This Hafema rapids ride had a brisque pace, a strong layout featuring a whirlpool, solid theming and a few absolutely drenching moments. Overall a very strong ride for a good line-up of water rides.

Unfortunately, we ere hit by two major ride closures during out visit. One being the very interesting looking dark-ride "Thor's Hammer" which would have offered a nice indoor experience on a cold october day. The other was the parks biggest flatride "SpinSpider" the parks big pendulum ride by Zamperla. With the parks only darkride and biggest flat-ride down, there really wasn't that much left for us to rave about, hence the low score.

We ate burgers with loaded fries at Rockburger in Route 66 and while our sets offered some amazing views of Speed Monster's launch, the food wasn't anything to write home about. It wasn't bad per say but it was about as standard as it could get, which feels a lot like Tusenfryd's food game in general. Variety and quality seemed just okay and the score reflects that.

Now given our food, our expectations for snacks weren't that high but we were left quite surprised with the quality of our two waffles with ice-cream and toppings that we got from "Ice cream palace" on the hill behind Loopen. They were a bit more complex and better tasting than usual waffles with ice-cream. The parks variety doesn't seem particularly big but this is an example where quality beats quantity.

The operations at Tusenfryd was pretty great. Not only did they have several trains running on all major coasters (except Storm which can only run one), but we were especially impressed with the way they handled a lot of unfortunate situations we experienced with unruly teens trying to queue jump, get in line after the queue was closed and trying to jump onto some security net and Speed Monster to retrieve a lost item. Dispatch times were okay but with no ride breakdowns, the park does really well here.

39.text:
Liseberg does very well in food. We got two meals during our 11 hour long visit, both of them fairly simple but hey, sometimes you need to check if the foundations are solid before you start building on top of it. Our first meal were Chicken Nuggets and fries at Korvhallen. It was alright and had some dippings like Aioli and Truffel dip.
Our dinner was  fish & chips at Skeppsmagasinet, another basic dish but this one was surprisingly solid and the surroundings were very relaxing which felt quite needed at this point of our visit.
Overall, while the overall food quality was nice but nothing special, we dsicovered even more of their food selection and realized that this park actually has a very strong food catalogue and therefore, Liseberg continues to do very well in this category.

We tried several minor snacks this time around, before ending with the ever-so-stable waffle at Systrarna Olsons Våffelbageri which is an absolute must-try snack at Liseberg. We tried some hot chocolate at Fröken Ellens Café, got some candy from the Helix store and of course ended with a nice pistache waffle which was as great as always, still our favourite snacks we have had. Overall, both quality and selection is good at Liseberg.

This is where the circumstances of our visit starts to rear its ugly head. Ride availability was hit hard during our visit and while most of it were closures, which doesn't affect the operations score, we did experience breakdowns on both Mechanica, Tempus and Lisebergbanan, where we ourselves got evacuated from the mid course brake run. Thankfully, only one of these didn't open again and we continue to be impressed by the general dispatch speed on the major coasters, running 2 or 3 trains and being quick with checking restraints, but we have experienced enough breakdowns and temporery closures over our 3 visits to problaim that Liseberg isn't the most reliable park when it comes to ride availability.

We visited Liseberg for the third time on the 25th of April, 2026 between 10:45-21:45 for a massive 11 hours at one of our favorite european parks. However, while many of Lisebergs strength, like its beauty, its strong attraction line-up and the great dispatches, still shined bright, this visit didn't exactly go as planned and Liseberg couldn't quite live up to the grand expectations we have for it.

Nothing much to add for this category, Liseberg remains a park that does it best to combat the somewhat impractical hillside that splits the park layout. They do this by having paths that are easy to navigate, ride entrances that are easy to find and of course, our favorite app that is super easy to use and has lots of good information about the park and the contents of it. While it can be a bit exhausting having to decide when you take the trip up the escalators (or the straining walk if you want), the park really does the best with what its given, and it really shouldn't be the practicaility of the park that hurt your visit to Liseberg

Liseberg continues to charm with its mix of nature (the hillside, tress and small pond that runds through the middle of it) and the well themed attractions. The park strikes a great balance between charming inner-city amusement park and detailed themepark. There are also no shortages of amazing views within this park as you can see the most of Liseberg and a good amount of the surrounding Göteborg from a dozen different attractions. While the segregation of theming is fairly basic and it doesn't bolster deep immersion, Liseberg is a beautiful, lively, yet very comfortable park to spend a day in.

Unfortunately, this is where Liseberg really dropped the ball during our third visit. We had major problems on our first visit with Helix being closed on top of several other major attractions being closed for the halloween season but Liseberg completely redeemed itself on our second visit, having everything remaining open after the staggered opening period. Therefore, we fully expected this may visit to go fairly smooth but it was anything but. After the first 45 minutes in the park we had gotten 4 rides on Helix, great! After over 3 hours in the park he had... the same 4 rides on Helix and nothing more. Now, this was in part due to us standing in queue for Mechanica just to have it breakdown just before our turn as well as spending a solid 30 minutes at Lisebergbanan, getting evacuated from it. But it was also en large due to us spending time walking around the park, wondering why just so many attractions were closed. These were the rides that were closed for the entire 11 hours: Luna, Lisebergshjulet, Uppswinget, Underlandet, FlumeRide, Kållerado and then Stormvåg which hadn't opened yet. One thing all these rides have in common: they were ALL supposed to be open during the summer season. Liseberg were effectively running a park without a darkride, without a waterride, without their only true family coaster and with several other major flatrides closed. While we still need to remember just how amazing their free virtual queue system policy is, we have now had 2/3 visits be with several major ride closed for the entire day and the score has to reflect this, and so it will. We hope that our next visits will be more like our second visit, in this regard.

Helix remains the absolute star of Liseberg and is an absolutely fantastic rollercoaster, though it has dropped out of it's golden status. This is of no fault of Helix itself, it was just as great as the last time we rode it but after having rode Zadra and Voltron since our last rides, we simply realized that an even higher level of coaster goodness exists. But take nothing away from Helix, it is still one of the most complete coasters we've ridden and it offers the perfect blend of smooth and comfortable but also powerful and full of forces! It's true that it isn't a launch coaster that focuses on the launches itself but the amount of floaty inversions, extended hangtime and surprisingly strong airtime is something that most coasters can only dream of having. Helix stands strong as a true highlight in Europe.

The supporting coaster line-up of Liseberg is still very strong. Balder and Valkyria are two very strong supporting coasters and Lisebergbanan offers something very different from most modern coasters. Liseberg still definitely lacks a major, modern family coaster though the addition of Luna did help. Overall, it's not the deepest coaster line-up but it's a varied one, offering very different coaster types and they are all of very high quality.

We don't really have anything new to add as both FlumeRide and Kållerado Rafting were both closed during the entire day. Kållerado was completely drained of water for our full visit, and so was FlumeRide for the first half of the day, though we did just happen to be standing at the ride, looking at it, when they all of a sudden started filling it with water (which it did surprisingly quick might we add). Unfortunately, despite getting our hopes up, none of the two waterrides ever opened. Thankfully for Liseberg, since we have been on them just last year, they get to keep their previous score (at least in this category), but while the weather wasn't that great during our visit, we're still disappointed that both of the parks major water rides were closed for 11 hours straight.

Liseberg's flat-ride game continues to be of the very highest tier in Europe, though we had hoped to be able to actually confirm this by making the full round through the many great attractions, however, Uppswinget, Mechanica, Lisebergshjulet & Underlandet were all closed for the entire day (except Mechanica which broke down while we were in the queue for it and never opened back up again). We also just about missed out on Stormvåg, though this we did know beforehand.
Overall, because we have ridden this rides before, we can comfortably say that the ride line-up at Liseberg is incredible strong.

59.text: The food in Djurs Sommerland is okay. It has a decent selection though the options are quite basic like burgers, pizza, sandwiches and a food market place. The burger, nuggets and fries that we got was of decent quality but quite plain in terms of ingrediencies and taste. Overall, we get the feeling that Djurs is currently playing it very safe when it comes to their food offerings and we would like to see them put it a bit more effort here.

We visited Djurs Sommerland on April 16th, 2025 as the first park on our Jylland-based easter trip. Now, even though this is our first official trip to the park, it should be said that this is a park we've been to many times over the years, from childhood, to just a couple of years before this trip so this is a bit of a 'comfort park' for us. It's a very nice park but not without its shortcomings, so lets talk about it!

Perhaps the best thing about Djurs Sommerland is how well it is run. The park is well staffed, the staff is always friendly and offers great service. There is free parking, you can bring your own food and drinks into the park to eat at the many picnic tables and the merch game is very strong. There also wasn't a single pre-closed ride at our visit. We didn't have a single thing to complain about when it comes to how they run the park and it's one of the things that ensures you almost always have a good time at Djurs Sommerland.

Djurs Sommerland is a very nice park to walk around in and one of the reasons for this is it's pretty appearance with lots of greenery and a very traditional "Summerland" feel that it has maintained despite it now being so much more than just a summerland park. Now, it is a very flat park and doesn't have any huge structures to be wowed by or a pretty lake in the middle so it does feel a bit more plain than some other, more intricate parks but bottom-line is, it's a very nice and lively park with good theming and a comfortable atmosphere.

The best coaster race is often a tight one between the parks three Intamin coasters but usually we would expect Piraten to take the crown towards the end of the day when it's running at its peak. However, as you can see in the gallery, Piraten - alongside Skatteøen - actually broke down (mid-lifthill-evacuations and all) and therefore, we only got 3 rides on it, all of them being in the first half of the day and if you know anything about Piraten, it's that it really needs to warm up before it gets good. Therefore, we actually awarded best coaster to Juvelen which in itself is no slouch. It's a long layout filled with tight turns and a great boost launch into a genuinely intense turn afterward. While it is perhaps slightly repetitive and the 2 "camelbacks" doesn't give any air at all, it's still a very fun, smooth and overall satisfying coaster that takes the crown as Djurs best coaster but we're sure that the battle is going to be just as close on our next visit!

The supporting coaster line-up is overall solid. Djurs has a well varied collection of kids and family coasters, starting all the way at the tiny Jungle Rally and stretching all the way to a very large family-thrill coaster in Dragekongen. Piraten is their one thrill coaster, though it is a fairly accessible one. Overall, Djurs coaster line-up is good but there are still plenty of room for improvements especially regarding having a thrill coaster with inversions as there are currently not a single ride in the entirety of the park which offers that.

Just like their coaster line-up, Djurs sports a solid and varied collection of water rides though without sporting an actual great one. It has a decent log flume in Long Cun Expedition, an okay rapids ride in Rio Grande River and a fun water coaster in Skatteøen. While it is nothing to dive headfirst over, it is a fairly adequate set of attractions to combat the hot summer days (and they even have a mini-flume for the young ones). A 6.5 is in order.

Continuing with the theme of being above-average without truly making a splash, Djurs brings a fairly large collection of flat-rides to the table. While most of them are fairly basic and aimed towards a younger audience, they do have a few true standouts here. Tigeren is a genuinely amazing flatride, one of our favorites in fact and they also - as of 2024 - have an actual proper drop tower in El Gritto. The polyp known as 'Soulken' is an underrated ride and the tilting family droptower 'Sablen' is a unique attraction in itself. Djurs biggest downfall here is without a doubt the fact that they don't have a darkride at all. They used to have an africa-themed boatride but the closed we come now is their car-ride 'Dino Xpedition' which as a small dark ride section in it. The lack of a darkride is a huge, gaping hole for a themepark like this and it would rocket their rating upwards if they actually got one.

Now let me tell you about the snacks that we got here, oh boy...
We actually had fairly high expectations for the kind of snacks a nice park like Djurs would offer but what we were given was the equivalent of an icicle made of leather, painted brown and served on a stick. They looked nice, but never in our lives have we had so called "waffles" be so dry that it was actually inedible. It takes a lot for us not to eat waffles with chocolate on but Djurs actually managed to do it. Now the selection of snacks was quite decent so we don't want to place Djurs in the basement but next time, they need to do a lot better here!

Back to some positives! Djurs is a really practical park to navigate around. The layout is essentially a long square with two main paths on each side, connecting at the front and the back of the park, while having a few interconnecting paths between them. They also have a really good app, many facilities and easy-to-find entrances.

8 is a great score but Djurs was actually on the path of scoring even higher since the dispatches were fast and efficient and the staff were downright amazing in how they were dressed and interacted with people. We did see one train ops on Juvelen for the first half of the day but they later put in a second train. The big thing here is when both Piraten and Skatteøen had major breakdowns at the same time,  requiring mid-lifthill evacuations and the rides shutting down for the remaining time (there were about an 1 or so left anyway but were were hoping to catch some re-rides on Piraten before we left. Overall, this big blow-up at the end doesn't hurt their score more than it still should be clear that the operations at Djurs are great.

60.text: We visited Fårup Sommerland on april 17th 2025 as part of our domestic Jylland trip in our home country Denmark. This was our first official Parkrating visit and a park that neither of us had been to since we were kids. A lot has changed since then, especially for Fårup sommerland which has developed into one of the most exciting theme parks in Denmark.

One thing that has remained as Fårups biggest selling point, pretty much since its creation, is its location deep inside a forest. The park has a lot of natural beauty and an entirely different,much more relaxed atmosphere compared to a lot of other major themeparks.
The park feels very nice to be in and is kept very clean and tidy, though a lot of the scenery and ride-theme does becomes quite repetitive quite quickly. It would be nice to see them branch out and make the park more visually dynamic in the future.

Fårups biggest disadvantage by far is its practicality. While it does have a few nice features like pay-before-parking and a nice food-ordering app, its weird, unbalanced T-shaped layout and confusing signage means that you're going to spend a lot of time hiking back and forth between the major rides. The lack of connecting paths or any type of shortcuts mixed with a few hard-to-find entrances (Orkanen, I'm looking at you), means that it can be quite a frustrating experience, especially if it's your first time visiting the park.

One of the exciting new prospects that really helped Fårup in its coaster battle with Djurs sommerland, is Fønix. This is easily the parks best attraction and is genuinely a great ride. In fact, we believe it's currently the best coaster in the entire country! It's an impeccibly smooth and dynamic coaster with a very dynamic and fun layout, offering a plethora of different elements and forces. It's extremely re-ridable though it may not truly satisfy the most hardcore thrillseekers out there. You can read more about Fønix on its own dedicated page (by clicking its name).

Beyond their star coaster, Fårup offers a decent supporting lineup of coasters, the best-of-the-rest being 'Falken', the S&S woodie, designed by Alan Schilke. This coaster is a very fun, although fairly rough ride with some decent air time. Their Gerstlauer launch coaster 'Lynet' brings the power and intensity though we did find it quite uncomfortable between the big over-the-shoulder restraints and rough transitions. After that, the park sports several Vekoma family coasters, 'Orkanen' being the most exciting of the bunch. Overall Fårup has done fairly well in this category.

Given the woodland theme and close-to-nature setting, you really would think Fårup would be the kind of park to bring a strong water-ride lineup but we weren't particularily impressed with the offering at hand. "Træstammerne" is a Big Country Motioneering flume with three drops, almost identical to the one in Djurs Sommerland but none of them offering much in terms of thrills and it was quite lacking in theming aswell. Fårup Rafting is a Bear Rides rapids ride that was a nice, leisurely stroll but far too tame for our liking. We like our water rides to have some zip to them and give an appropriate amount of water but these two didn't really give that much.

When it comes to rides besides coasters, Fårup is definitely still quite lacking in this department. They have several of the common ones like a wave swinger, tea cups, a junior droptower and a few other family-sized flats but their small Zamperla suspended frisbee 'Hvirvelvinden' was left to carry the thrills. Luckily, it's quite a forceful ride. Fårup also doesn't really have a proper darkride, only an old and basic 4D cinema. Non-coaster rides aren't really one of Fårups strengths.

Now to one of Fårups biggest strengths - their food. It's quite clear that Fårup cares a lot about quality when it comes to food. They actively advertise it and fair enough, they sure can back it up. We ate at 'Spisekammeret' which is a food-hall type eatery with different types of food stalls inside. We got an extremely well cooked and high quality burger, very delicious. Fårup aslo has several other interesting food places, like 'Oasen - Social dining' which looks enticing for our eventual, second visit

When it comes to snacks, it was kind of a mixed bag. Once again, the park offers solid variety and seems to pride itself on quality. We got some nice, freshly made pancakes from one of the stalls in the park. However, we also tried their very own - and at the time - brand new ice cream dairy 'Brogaardens is' which unfortunately left us quite disappointed. One of us got a slightly above average ice cream but the other got a quite terrible, completely crystalized ice cream that tasted very off. We will be sure to their ice cream again so we can once and for all settle whether Fårup does have the goods but for now it's a stalemate and a 6.0 rating.

Ride operations were good at Fårup. The staff was very friendly and let people stay inside the trains if no-one was waiting at the station gate. This is the main reason why we were able to get 11 rides on Falken and 7 on Fønix in one day, while still exploring the park and its other rides. Some of the dispatches were a bit too relaxed and the employees did have a tendency to staple, which we don't enjoy but we do very much appreciate that not a single ride closure occurred despite it raining quite a lot that day.

Fårup is managed beautifully. They had all of their rides and facilities open, they had both free parking and free water stations throughout the park. They allowed access to the main square before the rope drop which is nice given the parks odd T-shape layout. We also really enjoyed their "everyone gets to go twice policy" they had on every coaster (probably because it was a quieter day). The only thing that really wasn't that good was their merch. But overall, we were very impressed with how the park was run.

61.text: We visited Tivoli Friheden on April 18th, 2025, from 15:30 to 21:30. As the overall score reflects, Tivoli Friheden currently stands as the lowest rated park we have visited. This does not make it a bad park outright, but being a small park that uses roughly a third of its space as concert space, it's a long way away from scoring high on our platform. Without a single standout category, it ends up feeling like a credit worth obtaining rather than a park worth revisiting.

Friheden is an inner-city amusement park that sits in the middle of Aarhus, not unlike Tivoli Gardens or Liseberg, but that is largely where the similarities end. Friheden is not an inherently beautiful park and only makes weak attempts at scattered theming, though it does have a more intimidate and energetic atmosphere than most themeparks. Besides, watching the sun set from the Ferris wheel or seeing Vindfald light up in blue and red at night shows clearly that this is a park best experienced after dark. It should also be said that this is not the most clean park and its cleanliness only got worse as the evening progressed. Expect good vibes, not immersive theming.

Vindfald is Friheden’s newest coaster, replacing the old, compact inverted coaster 'Cobra' which closed in 2022. We did actually have some expections going into this as it was a brand new Gerstlauer that surely must be miles better than the ride it replaced but we did leave, feeling a bit underwhelmed. A vertical lift hill is always welcome, and both the first drop and inversions deliver decent moments. Unfortunately, despite being a new ride, Vindfald is surprisingly rough, and aside from the lift hill, the ride is over before you know it. An extremely short  and semi-rough ride doesn't make for the best ride experience. Overall it's an okay coaster but we had hoped for more.

We cannot expect much from a park this small, but limited space should ideally lead to quality over quantity. Unfortunately, that is not the case here. Outside of Vindfald, the park offers two junior coasters that we do found too small to rate. That leaves Tyfonen, a Zamperla spinning coaster which, on our visit, barely even spun at all. So a spinning wild mouse that didn't spin and two junior coasters naturally means that we're far down on the scale.

This is an area where Friheden performs better than its coaster lineup. Himmelrum is a fun swing ride that had the potential to be genuinely great, though its impact is reduced now that the seats no longer spin freely as they did when it first opened. Hjertekig is Denmark’s tallest free fall tower, but is overall a weaker drop tower than other danish ones, such as El Grito at Djurs Sommerland or even the small 'cobratårnet' at BonBon-Land. Sky Tower offers a true free fall experience, albeit at an upcharge, and has become part of the park’s identity.
On the dark ride front, the park features both an interactive 5D cinema and an interactive zombie hunt dakride, which is a conversion from their old classic haunted house (and a downgrade at that). Unfortunately, neither experience left much of an impression and from here there's pretty much only a handful of kiddie flats left.
Had the experience of the drop tower matched its size, had the pendulum still had its fully rotating gondolas and has they still had a classic haunted house, the park most likely would have scored a lot higher.

Food quality at the park seemed decent, backed by a solid selection ranging from classic amusement park staples like burgers and pizza to sit down restaurants and a beer garden. We didn't see any unique food options but we had an above average burger and left with no real complaints, which earns this category a respectable score.

Snack selection was minimal, with only a few carts scattered around the park offering small assortments of candy or liquorice. The limited permanent food stands mainly served ice cream and churros. We tried the ice cream and were left disappointed, and the lack of variety keeps this score firmly below average.

We will keep this one brief. Crowd levels were low and queues were never more than 5 minutes so they can be excused for their somewhat slow dispatches. Staff was friendly and there were no ride breakdowns during our visit so overall the park did decent here.

Tivoli Friheden hosts concerts every Friday, included with the entrance ticket. While this added a fun and unexpected element to our visit, it also caused the park to quickly shift from theme park to concert venue, complete with visibly intoxicated and irresponsible teens throughout the grounds. To the park’s credit, the concert area was heavily staffed with both employees and security throughout the evening. Other issues still weighed down this score. While looking at a menu at one sit down restaurant, a waiter came by and removed it from the wall as we were looking at it and replaced it with a new one priced at nearly three times as much. Merchandise availability was extremely limited, and it was often unclear when or even if restaurants were open.
Overall, the management category - and pretty much our entire day - at Friheden can best be described as a mixed bag.

Unfortunately, Friheden doesn't have any actual water rides. They have some small boats you ride around in a lake but we don't count that as a water ride. Therefore, there really is only one score we can give in this category.

Tivoli Friheden is small enough park that you normally would expect quite a high score here, given the small footprint and while the park does have a simple T-shaped layout and is easy to navigate, basic information is oddly difficult to find. Restaurant locations were unclear, toilets were tucked away down dark alleys, and some rides were hidden behind the concert area, which becomes relevant later. Overall we can't say that it's a downright impractical park but we didn't feel like they utilized their inherent advantage of being a small park very well in terms of the natural flow of direction and ride placements etc.

77.text: This rating is twofold. While we encountered some truly great passenger interactions throughout the park, we also saw mediocre operations and even a few breakdowns, including our very first ride of the day were we had a pile-up on 'Søløven'. However, the base level of dispatches and staff-friendlyness was quite high so the final score ends somewhere in the middle but solid overall.

We visited BonBon-Land on July 12th, 2025, from 10:30 to 15:30. Even as we are writing this review, we still do not fully know what we experienced during those 5 hours. One thing is for sure, you won’t find this experience outside of BonBon-Land. Whether it was a good or bad experience is another question.

Also in the snacks department did BonBon-land do decent. We had some great loaded softice and some surprisingly high-quality bubble tea. Yes, there is definitely a difference. However, the selection was very limited and we really would have thought they would have had more unique options given their theming and the fact they cater to families but I guess they want people to buy their BonBon-candy from the store.

Where do we even begin? The park is truly unique when it comes to theming which is centered around BonBon-lands own set of weird and often wildly inappropriate characters. This can results in some truly unique ride-theming sometimes accompanied with their own theme song, which is great. But the backside of the coin is that for every unique and immserive set of theming we found, there was something equally as ugly, rundown and downright cursed, around the next corner. Some rides and areas were in desperate need of some TLC. We would love to see how a fully revitalized BonBon-land would look, given their unique style and emphasis on theming but for now it remains as an equally good and cursed experience.

BonBon-land has a really peculiar layout that pretty much forms a big circle but where the entry area is a path along the outside and then you enter from the north-west side of the park. Luckily, it is fairly easy to navigate given both how flat the park is and how many different connecting paths there are. However, several dead ends, no park app, no signage, and some odd ride locations land us at an average 5.

The best coaster in the park is Vild-Svinet, the very first Gerstlauer Euro-Fighter. While the ride is a historic one, the ride experience it gives is just okay. The vertical lifthill and first drop are great as always and the vertical loop is both smooth and fairly forceful. Unfortunately, the ride pretty much dies at that point, delivering only some jerky turns left and right before hitting the brakes. The ride is quite rough on the front row though surprisingly smoother in the back (not sure how that works but apparently this is not the only Euro-Fighter like this).
Overall, Vild-svinet is an okay thrill ride but it's best going into it, not expecting too much or you might leave a bit disappointed (and maybe with a headache as well).

BonBon-lands supporting coaster lineup is as weird as the rest of the park. Hankatten is a decent spinning coaster (if you love spinning it's downright great).Viktor Vandorm is a large custom Zierer Tivoli coaster placed around non-existing terrain with a station hanging over a pathway for no reason other than this was a relocation from a german park with lots of terrain. BonBon-land does of course have the legendary dog fart coaster which is below or rating criteria (but we give it 10/10 on the brown scale!). The supporting lineup exists but just barely at that.

The water rides of BonBon-land are okay and they lend themselves nicely to the parks theming. 'Bæver-rafting' is a decent Intamin rapids ride with a solid duration but a bit lacking in the rapids part. 'Vandrotten' is a very interesting self-built log flume with 3 average drops and some very unique theming. It is both impressive and perhaps a little bit cursed at the same time - BonBon-Land in a nutshell!

This category gets a major hit to its score as the parks Zamperla Giant Discovery 'Svend Svingarm', was down the entire day (and a few minor ones as well), leading to a slightly lackluster flat ride lineup during our visit. Thankfully, the drop tower (Cobra Tårnet) was a big surprise. This small Fabbri drop dower packs a crazy punch and is a true standout in the park. The parks only dark ride is self-built boat ride called 'Søløven'. It was...hmmm...interesting to say the least. It's clear that this park really needs their largest flatride to be open to be competitive in this category.

The food selection as BonBon-land was decent with burgers, pizzas and a Mexican buffet. You can also have your own barbeques at the park. We went with burgers which we initially thought were going to be bad given that they were quite cold but the taste was surprisingly good and saved them from a bad score. In the end, we were satisfied.

The in-park experience is really great, without any weird policies or facility closures. Add on good customer service, and BonBon-land was on its way to a high management score. The downside? Three flat rides were closed, one being their biggest one and we were only informed of this as we literally stood at the gate, about to enter the park. Overall, if you aren't hit with any of these closures, you should have a good time at this very weird danish theme park.

78.text: We visited Tivoli Gardens on July 12th, 2025 (17:00–24:00). Tivoli Gardens is essentially in our backyard, and we have visited on numerous occasions before ParkRating was created. However, during this trip, paying much more attention to the details gave us a newfound appreciation for the masterpiece that is Tivoli Gardens.

Just like Phantasialand, we had nothing to put a finger on when it came to appearance. Tivoli Gardens does its own thing, and it does it incredibly well. Every little detail feels thought out, and no matter where you are, there is always something that catches your eye. Pulling this off in the middle of Copenhagen truly deserves a 10.
Not much to say. Tivoli Gardens has no water rides, and here the park size cannot be an excuse. We have visited numerous smaller parks that have managed to pull off a great log flume or river rapids.

Operations throughout were up to par, with a lot of trains on the coasters leading to limited stacking and friendly, efficient staff. Seeing a 111-year-old wooden coaster being run to perfection with manual brakemen was a joy to see as a coaster enthusiast. As for flat rides, there were some slow operations, but nothing too bad.

Rounding it off, Tivoli Gardens is an extremely well-run park all around with no facility closures or other odd policies. The park is packed to the brim with staff (especially during the weekend when they run concerts), and we never felt any frustration or confusion during our 7 hours in the park. Only flaw? Tik Tak was closed.

Most often we rate practicality low when we rate appearance high, as most times one comes at the cost of the other. But for the first time, that was not the case. Tivoli (albeit helped by its smaller size) was logical and intuitive to navigate, with everything facing the main loop around the park. Only thing? Perhaps a few more signs to help. But even then, the app was also top notch.

This is the one area in which Tivoli Gardens is lacking, as you simply cannot build a massive GCI or Intamin coaster in a park this size (please prove us wrong). Even then, B&Ms Dæmonen delivers a solid ride with good intensity, great inversions, and backed up by Tivoli Gardens' appearance and theming, it creates a very pleasant ride. But nothing to write home about.

If we question whether Tivoli Gardens can pull off a single coaster, the question becomes even more troublesome when we look at the coaster depth. Dæmonen is supported by a smaller Mack family coaster (Mælkevejen) and then Rutschebanen, which has no right being as thrilling and smooth as it is, despite being built in 1914. So while not a bad lineup, it definitely lacks quantity and could use some more thrill rides.

Tivoli Gardens delivers when it comes to flat rides, with everything from thrills in Aquila, the Golden Tower and the Star Flyer, to alright dark rides in Minen and The Flying Trunk. (Room for improvement here.) And all of them are themed to perfection. Unfortunately, our presumed highlight in Tik Tak was closed when we visited.

Ever since we started our weird obsession with finding the best fast food burger, we’ve come up short again and again. That is, until we visited Gasoline Grill in Tivoli Gardens. Despite being a chain, they served up the single best burger we have ever tasted, with quality far beyond anything we have tried in any other park. (Ask for their signature sauce on the side, trust us.) Add on top the biggest and most diverse restaurant and food lineup we could have imagined, with over 60 solid food options. It truly shows that you do not solely come to Tivoli for the rides. You might just come for the food.

A park that delivers like Tivoli Gardens does on food is bound to deliver on snacks as well, and oh does it deliver. With everything from gourmet popcorn (the liquorice kind is amazing) to crazy selections of donuts, waffles, pancakes, ice cream, and anything else you might wish for on a hot summer day, Tivoli has got you covered. Sidenote: While we visited during the summer, going during the winter is highly recommended for their traditional Christmas food and snacks.

79.text: We visited Bakken on July 13th, 2025, 442 years after it first opened in 1583, making it the world’s oldest operating theme park. While Bakken has retained much of its charm and quirky character over the centuries, it also hides a few unexpected pearls among the chaos.

Walking through Bakken’s narrow Tivoli style streets filled with carnival games, cafés, restaurants, and odd little shops, it is hard not to smile. There is a genuine sense of history here, and in parts of the park it feels like very little has changed in over 400 years. That atmosphere alone does a lot of heavy lifting. Unfortunately, actual theming across the park is close to nonexistent. Every shop and ride seems to do its own thing, and many of the coasters look more like portable traveling fair attractions than rides designed for a permanent theme park.

Not much to complain about here. Bakken is laid out around a large loop that makes navigation incredibly easy. Finding rides, food, and facilities was simple, even without relying on an app or signage. It is refreshingly straightforward.

Remember those pearls we mentioned earlier? This is one of them. Tornado is not your standard Intamin spinning coaster. Instead, it feels like a rolling torture device that uses centrifugal force to personally insult every vertebra you own. The lift hill might as well be a forty five degree vertical launch based on how fast it pulls you up. And then there is Boost mode. Riding it in this setting made us seriously question the legality of the experience as we practiced full defensive riding. We will never ride it again. (Ok, we probably will). It is the most intense ride we have experienced in ParkRating history, and for that alone, we respect it immensely.

Starting with the positives, and as expected from a amusement park, Bakken’s flat ride selection is solid. Supernova is a great swing ride, Sky Roller is your typical Gerstlauer flier, and rounding things off with a Zamperla NebulaZ creates a well balanced lineup. Dark rides, however, are a different story. Quality here is rough and serves as a sharp reminder that parts of this park are very, very old.

We praised the atmosphere created by Bakken’s many food stalls earlier, and visually that still holds true. Unfortunately, not all of them deliver. We ended up eating at American Diner in search of our usual Season 1 and 2 burger. While the food itself tasted fine, the following hour and a half hunched over a theme park toilet decidedly was not. How does this still land at a 4.5? We choose to believe we were unlucky, as the overall food selection is extensive and many of the stalls we passed looked genuinely appetizing.

Rutschebanen was unfortunately closed during our visit, which leaves Mine Train Ulven as the main supporting act. Thankfully, it is a surprisingly good Intamin family coaster. Vilde Mus, however, stands as our lowest rated Mack ride ever, and as of 2026, that still holds true. Had Rutschebanen been operating, this score would have been significantly higher, as it is very much Bakken’s signature attraction.

Bakken only offers a single water ride, and as per ParkRating protocol, we expect at least two unique water rides to avoid heavy deductions in this category. That said, it might have been the scorching July heat, but we genuinely enjoyed the log flume Vandrutschebanen. Quantity hurts the score, not the experience itself.

While we did not get to experience Rutschebanen due to its breakdown during our visit, the rides we did manage to ride featured solid dispatches and friendly staff throughout. Nothing stood out as exceptional, but nothing caused frustration either.

Bakken offers a very classc selection of amusement park snacks and deserts, including ice cream, candy, cotton candy, bubble tea, and churros, just to name a few. We went with ice cream, which was completely standard but good, and a bubble tea that fell well below expectations. The variety available across the park does most of the work in keeping this score at a 6.5.

So where do all these highs and lows leave us? Bakken is a truly unique and historic park with hidden perks and standout attractions, but it also lacks several elements that traditionally define a modern theme park. Perhaps that is the point. Bakken feels like something else entirely, something that is difficult to categorize. Whatever it is, it is run competently. We saw no facility closures, no staggered ride openings, and no strange park policies. The biggest downside? No merchandise available in the world’s oldest theme park.

80.text:
If you’ve heard anything about Legendia, it’s probably regarding their 2017 Vekoma looping coaster, Lech Coaster. This is the one part of the park where we actually had some expectations for the ride experience. Overall, two things became clear after our four rides on Lech Coaster:

It’s a great ride.

It was running very sluggishly during our visit.

While the first drop was fantastic and the layout had several strong airtime moments, the tales of relentless pace and constant, strong positive G-forces were severely lacking. Not that they weren’t present at all, but they were not nearly as intense as had been foretold. We could really feel it by the end of the ride, which felt quite slow.

In terms of appearance, Legendia is a very mixed bag. There are definitely areas that look like they haven’t received much attention over the past several years, like standing-but-not-operating rides, closed rides that appear to have been idle for over a decade, and some fairly plain areas. However, Legendia does present genuine beauty in certain places, especially around their newer rides. Combined with the natural beauty of the lake in the middle of the park and abundant greenery, the park overall is decently presented.

Unfortunately, Diamond River - a very good looking modern flume ride - was closed. Therefore, this rating only include the park’s rapids ride, Dolina Jagi. This 2020 Hafema rapids ride was a calm, leisurely stroll and was just okay. In fact, we found it a bit boring, as we prefer rapids that have more zip, chaos, and water. If Diamond River had been open, this rating would surely have been higher.

Back to some positives! Legendia has a surprisingly solid lineup of flat rides, mainly for children and families, but also a few thrill rides. This even included a 1959 Zierer Flying Machine called Dream Flight Airlines, which was a very cool sight to behold. Another big surprise was their well-hidden interactive dark ride, Bazyliszek, a 2018 ETF Systems trackless dark ride. It was a lot of fun and probably one of the best dark rides we’ve experienced so far. Still, many of Legendia’s rides are smaller and older, so there’s still plenty of room for improvement.

This score should have been higher. There were few snack options in the park, so when the churro stall finally opened, we were set on it. However, it closed just a couple of hours after opening, so no churros for us. The only other available option was a cafeteria selling pre-made desserts like panna cotta, which was... far less satisfying than the churros we had hoped for

It can be quite hard to balance the weights of dispatch times and the number of trains in action versus the amount of people and the actual need for these things. Never has this been more true than at Legendia, where there simply weren’t any people for the majority of the day. Except for one other coaster enthusiast that we met, we were at times the only people riding the two major roller coasters. Therefore, it is simply impossible to rate the park’s operations, as we just don’t have sufficient grounds to give them a proper rating. Therefore, we default the rating to a 5.

Most of the other categories where Legendia has gotten lackluster ratings have been because of closures or temporary shortcomings. But when it comes to how they run their park, there are several parts that really did hinder us from enjoying the park to the fullest. First off, they have perhaps the worst website of any theme park ever, with outdated information and constant links to third-party websites. Secondly, they had a no re-ride policy that forced us to get out and walk the entire long and confusing queue of their rides, despite us being - quite literally - the only people there. They also had a lot of facility closures and a major ride closure (and several smaller ones that we were unsure of whether or not they were even still operating but then take them out so they aren’t eyesores). Overall, the park really hurt themselves here, and it’s a shame for a park that otherwise does have a lot of potential.

We visited Legendia on October 11th 2025, for around 5½ hours on our first-ever visit to this regional polish park. This was a true warm-up park before our grand two-day visit to Energylandia. Therefore, we came with rather low expectations, knowing very little about the park, and most of the things we had heard were negative. Overall, we came away thinking that Legendia has clear, unrealized potential. While the total rating ended up being around what could be expected, it wasn’t in the ways we anticipated. Let us explain!

Legendia doesn’t really offer much else in terms of roller coasters; in fact, they only have one. Since Dream Hunters Society closed in 2023 and Scary Toys Factory was standing SBNO, it all came down to Devil’s Loop, an old, relocated Soquet looper. It’s an interesting ride—especially with its backwards-facing seats in the back car—but I would be lying if I said it was a pleasant ride. Overall, Legendia could really use another roller coaster in the park.

For lunch, we got some pizza at Pizzeria Mia, located near the entrance. It was decent theme park pizza - slightly above average. Food variety in the park was rather scarce however, so a 5.5 rating seemed appropriate.

Legendia has a simple and practical layout: a large circle around a lake. It’s easy to navigate, though walking from one end to the other can be a bit of a hike. There were plenty of toilets around the park. The park didn’t have an app, but it didn’t really need one. One annoyance was the queues for the two roller coasters, but overall, it’s fairly practical to traverse.

81.text: Energylandia impressed us with incredible theming throughout. We had heard stories about the “front area” being poorly themed or even “haunted,” but aside from a few questionable uses of known IPs, the park was surprisingly cohesive. Many areas featured themed food, snacks, and even a park-wide soundtrack that was genuinely fantastic. The park was spotless too, as you’d expect from one this new.

There’s a high quantity of flat rides, including several solid thrill machines. Unfortunately, the park’s single dark ride is… something else. Imagine an old-school ride through a warehouse filled with random props that look like the interns’ latest TEMU purchases. or leftover Halloween decorations. When everything has a different theme, nothing feels themed.

We visited Energylandia on October 14th and 15th, 2025, for a two-day trip. The park managed to exceed all our expectations (including our very first RMC), offering a coaster lineup that we would argue no other park in Europe can beat. Combine that with solid scores across nearly every other category, and you have ParkRating’s first park to achieve an overall score above 9. We cannot wait to return.

Another well-deserved 11. With a lineup of 19 coasters, the sheer quantity is mind-bending. Sure, many are family or kiddie coasters, but the thrill segment alone is outstanding. Usually, we talk about parks having a “1–2 punch,” but here it feels more like a “1–5 uppercut combo.” The fact that Hyperion counts as a supporting coaster is criminal says it all. Add solid Vekomas like Abyssus, Formula, and Mayan, and it’s easy to see why Energylandia earns an 11 out of 10.

Energylandia checks all the boxes here. We found everything from classic theme park treats to some unique surprises, like a Dippin’ Dots-style ice cream from one of many vending machines scattered around the park. The waffle fries were also fantastic. Nothing we tried or saw justified a score below 9 in this category.

Energylandia’s food offering is massive, with plenty of variety and availability across the park. Day one gave us a surprisingly great kebab dish, but day two brought us a chicken meal that was dry enough to be used as kindling. We’re hoping it was just bad luck, so we’ll let the quantity, theming effort, and good first impression keep the score at 6.5.

Despite its enormous size, Energylandia is remarkably easy to navigate. Signage was clear, the app worked great, and we never once felt lost during our two full days at the park. The locker system took a little getting used to, but once we figured it out, it worked flawlessly. The only downside is that the lockers sometimes felt like an afterthought, requiring detours when exiting rides to retrieve our bags.

To round it all off, Energylandia is exceptionally well run. The free shuttle train to and from the parking area is a great touch, and for once, the park’s merchandise game was actually strong. The only minor flaw was that some facilities could have been better distributed throughout the park.

Five water rides — the most we’ve seen in any park so far. Intamin’s Speed packs a punch, but the rapids and flume rides fall somewhere between average and mediocre. Still, the quantity alone and the variety they offer easily secure a strong score.

Two full days. Zero breakdowns.
Every ride was well-staffed, and operations ran smoothly throughout. To hit a perfect score here, we’d need to see how the park handles larger crowds, but in an off season day in mid-October, everything ran like clockwork.

Zadra. Enough said. Ok, maybe a word or two.
Zadra delivers everything you could hope for from an RMC masterpiece, both in the front and back rows. There’s simply too much to praise in one paragraph , it’s that good. In short, this coaster stands tall at the very top of what Europe has to offer.

88.text: Europa-Park’s food selection is huge and cleverly tied to each country represented in the park, giving us three great days of meals from Croatian cevapcici to Austrian schnitzel. Eatrenalin also deserves a mention — we didn’t try it, but the idea of being driven through a Michelin-style experience by miniature Mack vehicles is wild. Most parks struggle with consistency over multiple days, but not here. Everything we ate was great, earning this category a strong (and rare) 10.

So far Europa Park has been pretty much perfect but finally we get to an area where the park doesn't shine. It's very clear that Europa Park is more than ready to sacrifice a simple layout in order to pack it with world class rides, great restaurants and amazing theming and the layout is genuinely confusing, made worse by an inconsistent (and sometimes non-working) app and very limited signage. There were plenty of ways they could have done more to help with the inherently confusing layout but they didn't. The high level of theming also hides some entrances, and more than once we figured out a ride existed because we saw the exit in a gift shop, only to then struggle to find the entrance itself. The big midways, free locker system and strong use of single rider lines are definite positives here, but for all the amazing things Europa Park is, practical is not one of them.

Mack, you son of a… you did it again. Voltron is the third coaster (and second Mack) to break the scale and take its place at the end of the table in the category for the best coasters we have ever ridden. With Voltron, Mack has somehow managed to create not one but two incredible rides merged into one perfect package. The first half is all about immense airtime and several zero-g sections, while the second half following the swing-launch lets Voltron show its teeth, delivering pure intensity and incredible pacing all around. It's the perfect contrast to its sister coaster Helix and they truly are two sides of the same, perfect coin.

The first of many categories where Europa-Park truly stands out. Its world-class theming and insane attention to detail make the comparisons to Disney more than warranted, all while being very different from the Disney parks. It perfectly mixes the classic and historic feel with the absolutely stunning modern theming like coasters lighting up the sky at dusk or some of the most immersive queues we have ever encountered (Voltron and Arthur especially stand out here). When all is said and done, there was not a single thing we could subtract points for. a flawless 11.

Let’s quick-fire this one. The fastest dispatches we have ever seen.
Three or more trains running even on a "quiet" winter day. impeccably efficient and with constant queue handling. Mack’s rolling-station concept they use on Arthur and Voltron has got to have its own shoutout as well for being a world-class innovation when it comes to improving the ride operations.

And the crown on top? Across more than twenty-four hours spent in the park, we did not experience one single breakdown on any ride. Not one.

This is an easy 11.

We tend to compare Europa-Park to Energylandia a lot, but quite frankly the numbers do not lie. We rated the coaster lineup in Europa-Park higher than any other park we have visited. Wodan night rides took the crown as our new favorite true wooden coaster, and Silver Star forgot its 20+ year age as it threw us over its hills at almost 80 mph. sprinkle on the ingenuity of coasters like Arthur, Eurosat and Euro-mir and Europa-Park proved there is nothing “Disney” about its coaster lineup.

Europa-Park is exceptionally well run, with consistently great service throughout our visit. Staff handled weather-related closures clearly and proactively, and the IT "grandma" fixing our app caching issues became an instant highlight. Merchandise game was strong, and we even saw rides continuing past official closing time, which was a welcoming surprise when trying to catch those final re-rides on Voltron. The only drawback is the smoking-allowed policy which we were not a fan of, but this does not come close to overshadowing all of the positives in what is yet another knockout category for this fantastic theme park.

Like the food, Europa-Park offers a huge snack selection themed to different countries alongside classic park treats. Fresh mini donuts with caramel sauce were an instant favorite, and the Scandinavian cafés kept us warm with hot chocolate and kanelbullar. The only miss was the crepes, which lacked flavor even without our questionable apple-sauce choice. Everything else impressed, leaving this category at a strong 9.

We visited Europa-Park on November 21st, 22nd, and 23rd, 2025, for an epic three-day winter trip with more than twenty-four hours spent inside the park. It is safe to say we experienced close to everything Europa-Park had to offer, and we were absolutely blown away.
Mack has created the blueprint for what a modern theme park should strive to be. It delivers, exceeds, and then keeps exceeding expectations at every corner, fully deserving the praise it receives within the theme park community.

As per ParkRating protocol, we can only rate what we rode, and during the winter season both Fjord Rafting and Atlantica were closed. What remained was a solid log flume in 'Tiroler Wildwasserbahn' with good theming and Poseidon, an extended version of watercoasters like 'Skatteøen' back home in Denmark, sporting two lifts and hills. While they were both solid, the limited quantity and lack of a standout water ride leave this category at a decent 6. It's scary to think there is yet another category that this amazing park would quite easily score higher in, were it not for the season we visited in. We are eager to return during the summer months to allow the park to truly shine even more.

We are used to an emphasis on flat rides with the occasional dark ride thrown into the mix, but Europa-Park completely pulled the rug out from under us with its incredible line-up of darkrides and simulation rides. It's a good think that we lump all darkrides and flatrides together into one category as this park has so many different types of rides that it would be near impossible to otherwise try and categorize them. We count a total of 9 darkrides in total (all Mack designs of course), one of them being the absolutely brilliant new revolving theatre 'Grand Prix EDventure' (sort of like the Disney classic!). We did miss out on Elfenfahrt, a small boatride, though this did look like the least of the impeccable darkride lineup. Piraten in Batavia was the true standout and without question the best darkride we have done so far. It will be fun to see how it stands up against the disney darkrides, once we get there. The park also has both a flying theathre (Voletarium) and a madhouse (Fluch der Kassandra).The only reason this is not a flawless 11 is due to the lack of thrill flatrides. Not that the park really needs them but it would be the one thing that would break the scale.

99.text:
Legoland has a decent ride collection, mainly centered around its four dark rides. The oldest is Piratbåde, an old Mack boat ride.
