We were really impressed with what great condition this was in, and it's clear the park takes excellent care of it. The park also has two interactive dark rides: Templet, a classic shooter with plenty of targets that all seemed to be working and cars you could manually spin (which also features a fun spinning tunnel), and NINJAGO The Ride, which had you shooting through hand motions. This mechanic was quite tough but also very fun.

Lastly, they have a flying theatre in Emmet's Flying Adventure, which was the best one we've done so far. It actually used the technology to immerse guests in a fun and chaotic LEGO-based world rather than just functioning as a complex commercial for real-life destinations.

The reason this score isn't higher is that the park doesn't have any true thrill flats anymore, as they chose to remove their KUKA robot arm ride, Ice Pilots School. The kids' and family collection is quite impressive, but the only thrills you can get from a flat ride now come from the manually controlled seats on Apocalypseburg Sky Battle, a Technical Park Aerobat.

We already knew beforehand that Legoland was not going to provide us with a big thrill machine to crown "The King of Billund," but the race for best coaster still ended up being quite close. In the end, Polar X-plorer, the highly unique polar-themed family coaster, walked away with the title of Legoland's best coaster. This Zierer ESC coaster offers comfortable trains, the park's biggest drop, solid pacing, and decent forces in the first half, along with a highlight section inside a cave (which we talk more about in our full review).

These aspects help elevate Polar X-plorer above what you would normally expect from a family coaster in terms of excitement and uniqueness. It's a great ride up until the third act, which is undeniably dull, as the coaster unceremoniously limps across its footprint back to the station. Ultimately, Polar X-plorer is a fun, unique coaster and a strong reason to come visit the OG Legoland park in its home country.

Despite not having a single thrill coaster, the supporting lineup honestly wasn't bad at all, especially considering the park's target audience. Minifigure Speedway, the park's newest coaster, almost took the crown for best coaster from Polar X-plorer with its fun and energetic racing elements and a surprisingly forceful backward section. X-treme Racers is a solid wild mouse; since it is the large park model, it features the usual fun hairpin turns alongside a zippy drop. Flying Eagle is a compact family coaster with a really solid layout that runs a two-lap cycle with good surrounding theming.

Finally, Dragen is a classic dark ride and coaster hybrid with good theming and immersion, though it is very tame compared to the later Legoland dragon coasters around the world. Yes, the lineup may lack quantity and there are no true thrill coasters, but this family-focused roster is of decent quality. Each coaster brings something different to the table, which helps entertain the large family crowd while also keeping enthusiasts like us satisfied.

Between being a highly themed IP park and catering heavily to families, we were unsure if the park was going to lean more toward premium food or large family buffets. In the end, it kind of did both. We saw large, noisy family buffets where it was more about quantity than quality, alongside theme park staples like burgers, pizza, and hot dogs. However, places like the Saloon seemed to have a proper steak and barbecue selection. We ended up eating at Pirate Pita, where we got a chicken pita and a poke bowl; the food was of high quality, and the ingredients seemed incredibly fresh. Overall, the park struck a nice balance between easy family options, theme park classics, and slightly more premium foods.

One of Legoland's biggest strengths is its presentation and how it immerses its guests into the world of LEGO. As mundane as LEGO can feel as a toy in the real world, having your own product and self-created IPs to build an entire theme park around is a massive bonus. We love how different Legoland Billund feels compared to other European parks, and it's clear a lot of care was put into displaying the creativity and playfulness of LEGO. The base theming is also very good all around, especially around the old Dragon Castle, which provides a very impressive facade for an otherwise very mild family coaster and also works as the centerpiece for Pirate Land. The park has plenty of greenery to balance out the LEGO theming, as well as plenty of sounds to give energy to the different areas. Overall, Legoland Billund looks and sounds great and, most importantly, feels distinctly LEGO when you're in the park.

For snacks, Legoland's selection consists mostly of the usual suspects, such as ice cream, slushies, crepes, and cafeteria-style treats like cookies and muffins. They did have a few unique options, like cotton candy - which usually appears at classic amusement parks - and LEGO-themed chocolate, which we got from Dolly's Coffee Shop. They had a few variants available, and the quality was solid, but it was quite expensive for what was essentially 'just chocolate'.

The real winner here was the churros. We were lucky to get the very first ones from a fresh batch, and these were as warm, crispy, and perfectly doughy as churros can be. Adding some soft serve helped balance out the otherwise heavy snack. Overall, the quality of the snacks seemed high, and the selection was decent, with a few fun themed options.

Legoland doesn't have the best layout, basically taking the form of an elongated rectangle that is sort of split into two sections. The main area holds most of the older attractions, including the entrance plaza and all its shops and recreational buildings, alongside the ever-so-impressive Miniland, DUPLO Land, Imagination Zone, NINJAGO World, and LEGOREDO Town. Knights' Kingdom is the area that separates this main section from the eastern half, which includes Adventure Land, Polar Land, and THE LEGO MOVIE World.

While Legoland doesn't do a great job of creating a natural flow throughout the grounds, it's never frustrating to navigate thanks to large paths, good signage, and the amazing park app, which can guide you to any attraction with pinpoint accuracy. Toilets and amenities are also strategically placed with clear signage indicating when they are open. The park is fairly practical, but it would benefit heavily if they could somehow connect THE LEGO MOVIE World with LEGOREDO Town or NINJAGO World on the other side of the castle. This would create a more natural flow and an easier-to-understand layout, allowing the castle to truly act as the park's centerpiece.

We visited Legoland Billund on **May 14, 2026**, for our first official Parkrating visit. Even though this is a park we had both been to several times as kids, neither of us had been back since childhood, so we were excited to experience the park and look at it through a new lens. Since this is a family park with a heavy focus on kids, we didn't have the highest expectations for the thrill ride lineup. Luckily, we have plenty of other rating categories for Legoland to impress in, and they definitely delivered!

When we were planning the trip and buying tickets on their website, it dawned on us just how egregious Merlin are when it comes to up charges. Not only is the park itself the most expensive in the Denmark, but the sheer amount of fast passes and other add-ons being sold across different tiers really put us off, especially considering that this is a park catering to families and young children.
We were really nervous that this corporate approach would spill over and hurt our in-park experience, but honestly, once inside the park, it was smooth sailing. Barely anybody used the fast passes, and advertising was kept to a minimum.

We saw no closed amenities, the staff was incredibly nice, and all attractions were up and running as soon as the park opened its doors. The merchandise game is also very strong, as one might suspect. Luckily, it wasn't just overpriced LEGO being sold; the park had plenty of T-shirts, IP-based toys, and mugs to choose from, and we always value a strong merch lineup.
It is really only the approach to fast passes and ticket tiers, as well as the expensive parking, that holds this score back from reaching the very top.

For snacks, Legoland's selection consists mostly of the usual suspects, such as ice cream, slushies, crepes, and cafeteria-style treats like cookies and muffins. They did have a few unique options, like cotton candy - which usually appears at classic amusement parks - and LEGO-themed chocolate, which we got from Dolly's Coffee Shop. They had a few variants available, and the quality was solid, but it was quite expensive for what was essentially 'just chocolate'.

The real winner here was the churros. We were lucky to get the very first ones from a fresh batch, and these were as warm, crispy, and perfectly doughy as churros can be. Adding some soft serve helped balance out the otherwise heavy snack. Overall, the quality of the snacks seemed high, and the selection was decent, with a few fun themed options.

While the addition of Minifigure Speedway helped make the coaster lineup more competitive and is an overall positive addition to the park, it did replace another standout attraction: the park's Intamin river rapids ride, Vikings River Splash. This was a fairly unique water ride featuring an elevator lift and a large drop. Its absence can definitely be felt, as this leaves Legoland with just a single water ride: a log flume called LEGO Canoe.

It's a fairly small flume ride with only a single drop. Though it takes a nice, scenic route with good theming and tranquil vibes, we can't imagine it's enough to satisfy the usually water-ride-crazed Scandinavian family crowd. Needless to say, this is an obvious weak point for the park. It is quite disappointing considering how well these types of rides pair with heavy theming, which is exactly what Legoland is all about. We really hope to see the park expand its water ride lineup and score a lot higher in this category in the future.

Legoland had great operations. While the park does have quite a lot of low-capacity rides and the dispatches were just okay, we saw no actual ride breakdowns during our visit—apart from a quick e-stop on Dragen—and several other things impressed us mightily.

First of all, the staff was super friendly and interactive. This makes sense given the target audience, but we still found the service to be a level above what we normally expect. Whether it was the innocent banter and cheering at Minifigure Speedway whenever one side won or lost, or the employees in Pirate Land playfully engaging with the younger kids, the staff made sure everyone had a great time.

Another brilliant approach to queue management was the inclusion of LEGO play areas directly inside the lines. This allowed kids to play and build while their parents held their spot in the queue. Sure, it might have made the lines slightly more chaotic with kids dipping in and out, but this also kept the number of tired, crying children in the actual queue to an absolute minimum.
